content: The equation $$\sqrt 3 \sin x + \cos x = 4$$ has
explanation:
$${{\sqrt 3 } \over 2}\sin x + {1 \over 2}\cos x = 2$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sin x\cos {\pi \over 6} + \cos x\,.\,\sin {\pi \over 6} = 2$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sin \left( {x + {\pi \over 6}} \right) = 2$$

This is not possible because value of sin x lies between $$-$$1 and 1. So no solution.

content: If $$\tan \left( {{{\alpha \pi } \over 4}} \right) = \cot \left( {{{\beta \pi } \over 4}} \right)$$ then
explanation:
$$\tan \left( {{{\alpha \pi } \over 4}} \right) = \cot \left( {{{\beta \pi } \over 4}} \right) = \tan \left( {{\pi \over 2} - {{\beta \pi } \over 4}} \right)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{\alpha \pi } \over 4} = n\pi + {\pi \over 2} - {{\beta \pi } \over 4},\,n \in I$$

$$ \Rightarrow (\alpha + \beta ) = 4n + 2 = 2(2n + 1)$$.

content: The principal value of $${\sin ^{ - 1}}\tan \left( { - {{5\pi } \over 4}} \right)$$ is
explanation:
$${\sin ^{ - 1}}\tan \left( { - {{5\pi } \over 4}} \right) = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( { - \tan {{5\pi } \over 4}} \right)$$ ($$\because$$ $$\tan ( - \theta ) = - \tan \theta $$)

$$ = - {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\tan \left( {\pi + {\pi \over 4}} \right)} \right)$$ ($${\sin ^{ - 1}}( - x) = - {\sin ^{ - 1}}x$$)

$$ = - {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\tan {\pi \over 4}} \right) = - {\sin ^{ - 1}}(1) = - {\pi \over 2}$$.

content: The value of $$\cos {\pi \over {15}}\cos {{2\pi } \over {15}}\cos {{4\pi } \over {15}}\cos {{8\pi } \over {15}}$$ is
explanation:
$$\cos {\pi \over {15}}\cos {{2\pi } \over {15}}\cos {{4\pi } \over {15}}\cos {{8\pi } \over {15}}$$

$$ = {1 \over {2\sin {\pi \over {15}}}}\left( {2\sin {\pi \over {15}}\cos {\pi \over {15}}} \right)\cos {{2\pi } \over {15}}\cos {{4\pi } \over {15}}\cos {{8\pi } \over {15}}$$ ($$\because$$ $$2\sin \theta \cos \theta = \sin 2\theta $$)

$$ = {1 \over {4\sin {\pi \over {15}}}}\left( {2\sin {{2\pi } \over {15}}\cos {{2\pi } \over {15}}} \right)\cos {{4\pi } \over {15}}\cos {{8\pi } \over {15}}$$

$$ = {1 \over {8\sin {\pi \over {15}}}}\left( {2\sin {{4\pi } \over {15}}\cos {{4\pi } \over {15}}} \right)\cos {{8\pi } \over {15}}$$

$$ = {1 \over {16\sin {\pi \over {15}}}}2\sin {{8\pi } \over {15}}\cos {{8\pi } \over {15}}$$

$$ = {{\sin \left( {\pi + {\pi \over {15}}} \right)} \over {16\sin {\pi \over {15}}}} = {{ - \sin {\pi \over {15}}} \over {16\sin {\pi \over {15}}}} = - {1 \over {16}}$$ ($$\because$$ $$\sin (\pi + \theta ) - \sin \theta $$)

$$\therefore$$ x = 1, $$-$$1, 2, $$-$$2 are four roots.

content: The equation $$\sqrt 3 \sin x + \cos x = 4$$ has
explanation:
$$\sqrt 3 \sin x + \cos x = 4$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{\sqrt 3 } \over 2}\sin x + {1 \over 2}\cos x = 2$$

This equation has no solution as

$$ - 1 \le \cos \left( {x - {\pi \over 3}} \right) \le 1$$.

content: The value of $$\cos 15^\circ \cos 7{1 \over 2}^\circ \sin 7{1 \over 2}^\circ $$ is
explanation:
$$\cos 15^\circ \cos 7{1 \over 2}^\circ \sin 7{1 \over 2}^\circ $$

$$ = {1 \over 2}\cos 15^\circ (2\cos 7{1 \over 2}^\circ \sin 7{1 \over 2}^\circ )$$

$$ = {1 \over 2}\cos 15^\circ \sin 15^\circ = {1 \over 4}(2\sin 15^\circ \cos 15^\circ )$$ ($$\because$$ $$2\sin A\cos A = \sin 2A$$)

$$ = {1 \over 4}\sin 30^\circ = {1 \over 8}$$

content: General solution of $$\sin x + \cos x = \mathop {\min }\limits_{a \in IR} \{ 1,{a^2} - 4a + 6\} $$ is
explanation:
$$\sin x + \cos x = \mathop {\min }\limits_{a \in IR} \{ 1,{a^2} - 4a + 6\} $$

Here, $${a^2} - 4a + 6 = {a^2} - 4a + 4 + 2 = {(a - 2)^2} + 2 \ge 2$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\sin x + \cos x = 1$$

Now, $${1 \over {\sqrt 2 }}\sin x + {1 \over {\sqrt 2 }}\cos x = {1 \over {\sqrt 2 }}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sin \left( {x + {\pi \over 4}} \right) = {1 \over {\sqrt 2 }} = \sin {\pi \over 4}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$x + {\pi \over 4} = n\pi + {( - 1)^n}{\pi \over 4}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$x = n\pi + {( - 1)^n}{\pi \over 4} - {\pi \over 4}$$

content: The value of $$\left( {1 + \cos {\pi \over 6}} \right)\left( {1 + \cos {\pi \over 3}} \right)\left( {1 + \cos {{2\pi } \over 3}} \right)\left( {1 + \cos {{7\pi } \over 6}} \right)$$ is
explanation:
$$\left( {1 + \cos {\pi \over 6}} \right)\left( {1 + \cos {\pi \over 3}} \right)\left( {1 + \cos {{2\pi } \over 3}} \right)\left( {1 + \cos {{7\pi } \over 6}} \right)$$

$$ = \left( {1 + {{\sqrt 3 } \over 2}} \right)\left( {1 + {1 \over 2}} \right)\left( {1 - {1 \over 2}} \right)\left( {1 - {{\sqrt 3 } \over 2}} \right)$$

$$ = \left( {1 - {3 \over 4}} \right)\left( {1 - {1 \over 4}} \right) = {3 \over {16}}$$.

content: $$P = {1 \over 2}{\sin ^2}\theta + {1 \over 3}{\cos ^2}\theta $$, then
explanation:
$$P = {1 \over 2}{\sin ^2}\theta + {1 \over 3}(1 - {\sin ^2}\theta ) = {1 \over 3} + {1 \over 6}{\sin ^2}\theta $$

$$0 \le {\sin ^2}\theta \le 1 \Rightarrow {1 \over 3} \le P \le {1 \over 2}$$

content: A positive acute angle is divided into two parts whose tangents are 1/2 and 1/3. Then the angle is
explanation:
Let $$\alpha$$ + $$\beta$$ = $$\theta$$ where tan $$\alpha$$ = $${1 \over 2}$$, tan $$\beta$$ = $${1 \over 3}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\tan \theta = \tan (\alpha + \beta ) = {{\tan \alpha + \tan \beta } \over {1 - \tan \alpha \tan \beta }}$$

$$ = {{{1 \over 2} + {1 \over 3}} \over {1 - {1 \over 2}.{1 \over 3}}} = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow \theta = {\pi \over 4}$$

content: The smallest value of $$5\cos \theta + 12$$ is
explanation:
$$\because$$ $$ - 1 \le \cos \theta \le 1 \Rightarrow - 5 \le 5\cos \theta \le 5$$

$$ \Rightarrow 12 - 5 \le 5\cos \theta + 12 \le 5 + 12$$

$$ \Rightarrow 7 \le 5\cos \theta + 12 \le 17$$

$$\therefore$$ Smallest value of $$5\cos \theta + 12$$ is 7

content: In triangle ABC, a = 2, b = 3 and sin A = 2/3, thne B is equal to
explanation:
Apply $${{\sin A} \over a} = {{\sin B} \over b}$$ (sine Rule)

$${{2/3} \over 2} = {{\sin B} \over 3} \Rightarrow \sin B = 1 = \sin 90^\circ $$

$$ \Rightarrow B = 90^\circ $$

content: Simplest form of $${2 \over {\sqrt {2 + \sqrt {2 + \sqrt {2 + 2\cos 4x} } } }}$$ is
If $$5\cos 2\theta + 2{\cos ^2}\theta /2 + 1 = 0$$, when $$(0 < \theta < \pi )$$, then the values of $$\theta$$ are
explanation:
$$5\cos 2\theta + 2{\cos ^2}{\theta \over 2} + 1 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 5(2{\cos ^2}\theta - 1) + (1 + \cos \theta ) + 1 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 10{\cos ^2}\theta + \cos \theta - 3 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow \cos \theta = {1 \over 2}$$ or $$ - {3 \over 5}$$

If $$\cos \theta = {1 \over 2} \Rightarrow \theta = {\pi \over 3}$$,

If $$\cos \theta = - {3 \over 5} \Rightarrow \theta = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( { - {3 \over 5}} \right) = \pi - {\cos ^{ - 1}}{3 \over 5}$$

content: The value of $${{\sin 55^\circ - \cos 55^\circ } \over {\sin 10^\circ }}$$ is
explanation:
$${{\sin 55^\circ - \cos (90^\circ - 35^\circ )} \over {\sin 10^\circ }} = {{\sin 55^\circ - \cos 35^\circ } \over {\sin 10^\circ }}$$

$$ = {{2\cos 45^\circ \sin 10^\circ } \over {\sin 10^\circ }} = 2{1 \over {\sqrt 2 }} = \sqrt 2 $$

content: The value of $${{\cot x - \tan x} \over {\cot 2x}}$$ is
explanation:
$${{\cot x - \tan x} \over {\cot 2x}} = {{1 - {{\tan }^2}x} \over {\tan x}}.\,\tan x$$

$$ = 2{1 \over {\left( {{{2\tan x} \over {1 - {{\tan }^2}x}}} \right)}}\tan 2x = {2 \over {\tan 2x}}.\,\tan 2x = 2$$

explanation:
2y = 1 ..... (i)

y = sinx .... (ii)

On solving, $$\sin x = {1 \over 2}$$, which has 4 solutions in $$ - 2\pi \le x \le 2\pi $$

content: The value of $${{\cot 54^\circ } \over {\tan 36^\circ }} + {{\tan 20^\circ } \over {\cot 70^\circ }}$$ is
explanation: $${{\cot 54^\circ } \over {\tan 36^\circ }} + {{\tan 20^\circ } \over {\cot 70^\circ }} = {{\tan 36^\circ } \over {\tan 36^\circ }} + {{\cot 70^\circ } \over {\cot 70^\circ }} = 2$$

content: If $$\sin 6\theta + \sin 4\theta + \sin 2\theta = 0$$, then the general value of $$\theta$$ is
explanation:
$$(\sin 6\theta + \sin 2\theta ) + \sin 4\theta = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2\sin 4\theta \cos 2\theta + \sin 4\theta = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sin 4\theta (2\cos 2\theta + 1) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sin 4\theta = 0$$ or, $$2\cos 2\theta + 1 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 4\theta = n\pi $$ or, $$\cos 2\theta = - {1 \over 2} = \cos {{2\pi } \over 3}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \theta = {{n\pi } \over 4}$$ or, $$2\theta = 2n\pi \pm {{2\pi } \over 3}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \theta = {{n\pi } \over 4}$$ or, $$\theta = n\pi \pm {\pi \over 3}$$

content: If $$\sin \theta = {{2t} \over {1 + {t^2}}}$$ and $$\theta$$ lies in the second quadrant, then cos $$\theta$$ is equal to
The number of solutions of $$2\sin x + \cos x = 3$$ is
explanation:
$$\sqrt 5 \left( {{2 \over {\sqrt 5 }}\sin x + {1 \over {\sqrt 5 }}\cos x} \right) = 3$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sin (x + \alpha ) = {3 \over {\sqrt 5 }}$$ [where $$\cos \alpha = {2 \over {\sqrt 5 }}$$, $$\sin \alpha = {1 \over {\sqrt 5 }}$$]

But, $$\sin (x + \alpha ) \ne {3 \over {\sqrt 5 }}( > 1)$$

$$\therefore$$ No solution.

content: Let $$\tan \alpha = {a \over {a + 1}}$$ and $$\tan \beta = {1 \over {2a + 1}}$$ then $$\alpha + \beta $$ is
explanation:
$$\tan (\alpha + \beta ) = {{{a \over {a + 1}} + {1 \over {2a + 1}}} \over {1 - \left( {{a \over {a + 1}}} \right)\left( {{1 \over {2a + 1}}} \right)}} = {{2{a^2} + a + a + 1} \over {2{a^2} + 3a + 1 - a}}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\alpha + \beta = {\pi \over 4}$$

content: If $$\theta$$ + $$\phi$$ = $$\pi$$/4, then (1 + tan $$\theta$$) (1 + tan $$\phi$$) is equal to
explanation:
$$\because$$ $$\theta + \phi = {\pi \over 4}$$

$$\therefore$$ $${{\tan \theta + \tan \phi } \over {1 - \tan \theta \tan \phi }} = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow \tan \theta + \tan \phi = 1 - \tan \theta \tan \phi $$

$$ \Rightarrow \tan \theta + \tan \phi + \tan \theta \tan \phi = 1$$

$$\therefore$$ $$(1 + \tan \theta )(1 + \tan \phi ) = 2$$

content: If $$\sin \theta + \cos \theta = 0$$ and $$0 < \theta < \pi $$, then $$\theta$$ =
explanation:
$$\sin \theta = - \cos \theta \Rightarrow \tan \theta = - 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow \theta = {{3\pi } \over 4}$$, in $$0 < \theta < \pi $$.

content: The value of cos 15$$^\circ$$ $$-$$ sin 15$$^\circ$$ is
explanation:
$$\sqrt 2 \left( {\cos 15^\circ {1 \over {\sqrt 2 }} - \sin 15^\circ {1 \over {\sqrt 2 }}} \right)$$

$$ = \sqrt 2 \cos (45^\circ + 15^\circ ) = {1 \over {\sqrt 2 }}$$

content: Let $f_n(x)=\tan \frac{x}{2}(1+\sec x)(1+\sec 2 x) \ldots\left(1+\sec 2^n x\right)$, then
explanation:
Telescoping product → a double-angle

One shows by induction (or by repeated half-angle/angle-doubling algebra) that

$$f_n(x)\;=\;\tan\frac{x}{2}\,(1+\sec x)\,(1+\sec2x)\,\cdots\,(1+\sec2^n x) \;=\;\tan\bigl(2^n x\bigr)\,. $$

Plug in $x=\tfrac\pi{16}$:

$$f_n\!\bigl(\tfrac\pi{16}\bigr) =\tan\!\bigl(2^n\cdot\tfrac\pi{16}\bigr) =\tan\!\bigl(\tfrac{2^n\pi}{16}\bigr)\,. $$

Solve

We want $\tan(\tfrac{2^n\pi}{16})=1$. That means

$$\frac{2^n\pi}{16}=\frac\pi4+k\pi\,,\quad k\in\Bbb Z \quad\Longrightarrow\quad2^n=16\bigl(k+\tfrac14\bigr)=4(4k+1)\,. $$

The only power of two of the form $4m+1$ is $1$ itself, giving

$ 2^n=4\quad\Longrightarrow\quad n=2,\;k=0. $

Hence

$$f_2\bigl(\tfrac\pi{16}\bigr)=\tan\!\bigl(\tfrac{2^2\pi}{16}\bigr) =\tan\!\bigl(\tfrac\pi4\bigr)=1.$$

Answer: Option D.

content: If $\cos (\theta+\phi)=\frac{3}{5}$ and $\sin (\theta-\phi)=\frac{5}{13}, 0<\theta, \phi<\frac{\pi}{4}$, then $\cot (2 \theta)$ has the value
explanation:
Given,

$\cos (\theta+\phi)=\frac{3}{5}$ and $\sin (\theta-\phi)=\frac{5}{13}$, with $0<\theta, \phi<\frac{\pi}{4}$

Since, $0<\theta+\phi<\frac{\pi}{2}, \sin (\theta+\phi)-\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)^2}=\frac{4}{5}$.

Since, $-\frac{\pi}{4}<\theta-\phi<\frac{\pi}{4}, \cos (\theta-\phi)=\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{5}{13}\right)^2}=\frac{12}{13}$.

We want to find $\cot (2 \theta)$. Use the identity $2 \theta=(\theta+\phi)+(\theta-\phi)$

$\sin (2 \theta)=\cos (\theta+\phi) \cos (\theta-\phi)+\cos (\theta+\phi)$$\sin (\theta-\phi)$

$\sin (2 \theta)=\left(\frac{4}{5}\right)\left(\frac{12}{13}\right)+\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)\left(\frac{5}{13}\right)=\frac{48}{65}+\frac{15}{65}=\frac{63}{65}$.

$\cos (2 \theta)=\sin (\theta+\phi) \cos (\theta-\phi)-\sin (\theta+\phi) \sin (\theta-\phi)$

$\cos (2 \theta)=\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)\left(\frac{12}{13}\right)-\left(\frac{4}{5}\right)\left(\frac{5}{13}\right)=\frac{36}{65}-\frac{20}{65}=\frac{16}{65}$

$\cot (2 \theta)=\frac{\cos (2 \theta)}{\sin (2 \theta)}=\frac{16 / 65}{63 / 65}=\frac{16}{63}$

content: The expression $$\cos ^2 \phi+\cos ^2(\theta+\phi)-2 \cos \theta \cos \phi \cos (\theta+\phi)$$ is
explanation:
To determine whether the expression $$\cos ^2 \phi + \cos ^2(\theta + \phi) - 2 \cos \theta \cos \phi \cos (\theta + \phi)$$ depends on $$\theta$$ and $$\phi$$, we need to simplify it and check for any dependencies on these variables.

Let's rewrite the expression:

$$\cos^2 \phi + \cos^2(\theta + \phi) - 2 \cos \theta \cos \phi \cos (\theta + \phi)$$

Consider the trigonometric identity for cosine of a sum:

$$\cos (\theta + \phi) = \cos \theta \cos \phi - \sin \theta \sin \phi$$

However, using this identity directly seems cumbersome. Instead, let's look at another potential approach, focusing on the structure of the expression. Notice that it is a combination of squared cosines and a product of cosines. If we consider transforming the expression into terms of squares and products, we might be able to discern its dependencies more easily.

Rewriting the expression in another form to recognize a potential independence can help. Let us test whether it can be simplified to a more recognizable form. After considerable algebraic manipulation, you would find it simplifies to:

$$\cos ^2 \phi + \cos ^2(\theta + \phi) - 2 \cos \theta \cos \phi \cos (\theta + \phi) = \sin^2 \theta$$

Thus, the given expression simplifies to:

$$\sin^2 \theta$$

From this result, it’s clear that the simplified expression only depends on $$\theta$$.

Hence, the correct option is:

Option B: independent of $$\phi$$

content: If $$0< \theta<\frac{\pi}{2}$$ and $$\tan 3 \theta \neq 0$$, then $$\tan \theta+\tan 2 \theta+\tan 3 \theta=0$$ if $$\tan \theta \cdot \tan 2 \theta=\mathrm{k}$$ where $$\mathrm{k}=$$
If $$A$$ and $$B$$ are acute angles such that $$\sin A=\sin ^2 B$$ and $$2 \cos ^2 A=3 \cos ^2 B$$, then $$(A, B)=$$

If $${1 \over 6}\sin \theta ,\cos \theta ,\tan \theta $$ are in G.P, then the solution set of $$\theta$$ is

(Here $$n \in N$$)
explanation:
Given, $$\frac{1}{6} \sin \theta, \cos \theta, \tan \theta$$ are in GP.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \cos ^2 \theta=\frac{1}{6} \sin \theta \tan \theta \\ \Rightarrow & 6 \cos ^2 \theta=\frac{\sin ^2 \theta}{\cos \theta} \\ \Rightarrow & 6 \cos ^3 \theta-\sin ^2 \theta-1=0 \\ \Rightarrow & (2 \cos \theta-1)\left(3 \cos ^2 \theta+2 \cos \theta+2\right)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 2 \cos \theta-1=0 \quad \left[\because 3 \cos ^2 \theta+2 \cos \theta+2 \neq 0\right] \end{array}$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta=\frac{1}{2}=\cos \frac{\pi}{3} \Rightarrow \theta=2 n \pi \pm \frac{\pi}{3}$$

content: If $$(\cot {\alpha _1})(\cot {\alpha _2})\,......\,(\cot {\alpha _n}) = 1,0 < {\alpha _1},{\alpha _2},....\,{\alpha _n} < \pi /2$$, then the maximum value of $$(\cos {\alpha _1})(\cos {\alpha _2}).....(\cos {\alpha _n})$$ is given by
explanation:
We know,

$$(\cot {\alpha _1})(\cot {\alpha _2})(\cot {\alpha _3})\,...\,(\cot {\alpha _n}) = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow \cos {\alpha _1}\cos {\alpha _2}\,...\,\cos {\alpha _n} = \sin {\alpha _1}\sin {\alpha _2}\sin {\alpha _3}\,...\,\sin {\alpha _n}$$

Multiply $$(\cos {\alpha _1}\cos {\alpha _2}\cos {\alpha _3}\,...\,\cos {\alpha _n})$$ both side

$${(\cos {\alpha _1}\cos {\alpha _2}\,...\,\cos {\alpha _n})^2}$$

$$ = (\cos {\alpha _1}\sin {\alpha _1})(\cos {\alpha _2}\sin {\alpha _2})\,...\,(\cos {\alpha _n}\sin {\alpha _n})$$

$$ \Rightarrow {(\cos {\alpha _1}\cos {\alpha _2}\,...\,\cos {\alpha _n})^2} = {1 \over {{2^n}}}$$

$$(\sin 2{\alpha _1})(\sin 2{\alpha _2})\,...\,(\sin 2{\alpha _n})$$

$$ \Rightarrow {(\cos {\alpha _1}\cos {\alpha _2}\,...\,\cos {\alpha _n})^2} \le {1 \over {{2^n}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \cos {\alpha _1}\cos {\alpha _2}\,...\,\cos {\alpha _n} \le {1 \over {{2^{n/2}}}}$$

Thus, the maximum value of

$$(\cos {\alpha _1},\cos {\alpha _2}\,...\,\cos {\alpha _n})$$ is $${1 \over {{2^{n/2}}}}$$

content: The equation 6<sup>x</sup> + 8<sup>x</sup> = 10<sup>x</sup> has
explanation:
Given, equation, 6x + 8x = 10x

$$ \Rightarrow {\left( {{6 \over {10}}} \right)^x} + {\left( {{8 \over {10}}} \right)^x} = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\left( {{3 \over 5}} \right)^x} + {\left( {{4 \over 5}} \right)^x} = 1$$

Let $${3 \over 5} = \sin \theta \Rightarrow {4 \over 5} = \cos \theta $$

$$\therefore$$ $${(\sin \theta )^x} + {(\cos \theta )^x} = 1$$

It is possible only x = 2

$$\therefore$$ given equation has exactly one real root.

content: $$\cos (2x + 7) = a(2 - \sin x)$$ can have a real solution for
Let f(x) = sin x + cos ax be periodic function. Then,
explanation:
Given, f(x) = sin x + cos ax

sin x is periodic with 2$$\pi $$.

f(x) is periodic only on sin x and cos x both are periodic.

$$ \therefore $$ cos ax is periodic at $${{2\pi } \over a}$$.

LCM of $${2\pi }$$ and $${{2\pi } \over a}$$ is possible only a is rational number.

Note :
(1) When $$n$$ is odd then the period of $${\sin ^n}\theta $$, $${\cos ^n}\theta $$, $${\csc ^n}\theta $$, $${\sec ^n}\theta $$ = $$2\pi $$

(2) When $$n$$ is even then the period of $${\sin ^n}\theta $$, $${\cos ^n}\theta $$, $${\csc ^n}\theta $$, $${\sec ^n}\theta $$ = $$\pi $$

(3) When $$n$$ is even/odd then the period of $${\tan ^n}\theta $$, $${\cot ^n}\theta $$ = $$\pi $$

(3) When $$n$$ is even/odd then the period of $$\left| {{{\sin }^n}\theta } \right|$$, $$\left| {{{\cos }^n}\theta } \right|$$, $$\left| {{{\csc }^n}\theta } \right|$$, $$\left| {{{\sec }^n}\theta } \right|$$, $$\left| {{{\tan }^n}\theta } \right|$$, $$\left| {{{\cot }^n}\theta } \right|$$ = $$\pi $$

content: If $${e^{\sin x}} - {e^{-\sin x}} - 4 = 0$$, then the number of real values of x is
explanation:
Given that, $${e^{\sin x}} - {e^{-\sin x}} - 4 = 0$$

Let $${e^{\sin x}} = t$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$t - {t^{-1}} - 4 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow t - {1 \over t} - 4 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {t^2} - 1 - 4t = 0$$

$$t = {{4 \pm 2\sqrt 5 } \over 2} = 2 \pm \sqrt 5 $$

Now, $${e^{\sin x}} = 2 \pm \sqrt 5 $$

$$ \Rightarrow {e^{\sin x}} = 2 + \sqrt 5 $$ and $${e^{\sin x}} = 2 - \sqrt 5 $$

$$ \Rightarrow {e^{\sin x}} = 2 + \sqrt 5 $$ [$${e^x} > 0$$]

Also, $$ - 1 \le \sin x \le 1$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${1 \over e} \le {e^{\sin x}} \le e$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${e^{\sin x}} \ne 2 + \sqrt 5 $$

Hence, there is no value of x.

explanation:
We know that, $$\sin 30^\circ = {1 \over 2} = 0.5$$

In 1st quadrant sin x is increasing function.

$$ \therefore $$ $$\sin 31^\circ > \sin 30^\circ $$

$$ \Rightarrow \sin 31^\circ > 0.5$$

content: If sin6$$\theta$$ + sin4$$\theta$$ + sin2$$\theta$$ = 0, then general value of $$\theta$$ is
explanation:
We have, sin6$$\theta$$ + sin4$$\theta$$ + sin2$$\theta$$ = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ sin6$$\theta$$ + sin2$$\theta$$ + sin4$$\theta$$ = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ 2sin4$$\theta$$ . cos2$$\theta$$ + sin4$$\theta$$ = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ sin 4$$\theta$$ (2cos 2$$\theta$$ + 1) = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ sin 4$$\theta$$ = 0 or 2cos 2$$\theta$$ + 1 = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ 4$$\theta$$ = n$$\pi $$ or cos 2$$\theta$$ = $${{ - 1} \over 2}$$ = cos$${{2\pi } \over 3}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$\theta$$ = $${{n\pi } \over 4}$$ or 2$$\theta$$ = 2n$$\pi$$ $$ \pm $$ $${{2\pi } \over 3}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$\theta$$ = $${{n\pi } \over 4}$$ or $$\theta$$ = n$$\pi$$ $$ \pm $$ $${\pi \over 3}$$, where n$$ \in $$z

content: The equation $$\sin x(\sin x + \cos x) = k$$ has real solutions, where k is a real number. Then,
explanation:
Let, $$f(x) = \sin x(\sin x + \cos x)$$

$$ = {\sin ^2}x + \sin x\cos x$$

$$ = {{1 - {{\cos }^2}2x} \over 2} + {{2\sin x\cos x} \over 2}$$

$$ = {1 \over 2} - {1 \over 2}\cos 2x + {1 \over 2}\sin 2x$$

$$ = {1 \over 2} + {1 \over 2}(\sin 2x - \cos 2x)$$

Now, $$ - \sqrt {{{(1)}^2} + {{(1)}^2}} \le \sin 2x - \cos 2x \le \sqrt {{{(1)}^2} + {{(1)}^2}} $$

[$$ \because $$ $$\sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} \le a\sin x + b\cos x \le \sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2}} $$]

$$ \Rightarrow - \sqrt 2 \le \sin 2x - \cos 2x \le \sqrt 2 $$

$$ \Rightarrow - {{\sqrt 2 } \over 2} \le {{\sin 2x - \cos 2x} \over 2} \le {{\sqrt 2 } \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over 2} - {{\sqrt 2 } \over 2} \le {1 \over 2} + {{\sin 2x - \cos 2x} \over 2} \le {1 \over 2} + {{\sqrt 2 } \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{1 - \sqrt 2 } \over 2} \le f(x) \le {{1 + \sqrt 2 } \over 2}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${{1 - \sqrt 2 } \over 2} \le k \le {{1 + \sqrt 2 } \over 2}$$

content: The order of the differential equation of all parabolas whose axis of symmetry along X-axis is
explanation:
The general equation of the parabola is x = ay2 + b, where a and b are arbitrary constants.

So, the differential equation is of order 2.

content: The area enclosed by $$y = \sqrt {5 - {x^2}} $$ and $$y = |x - 1|$$ is
explanation:
The graphs of $$y = |x - 1|$$ and $$y = \sqrt {5 - {x^2}} $$ are shown in the figure and the shaded region is the required region bounded by the two curves.



Let A be the area bounded by the given curves. Then,

$$A = \int_{ - 1}^1 {(\sqrt {5 - {x^2}} + x - 1)dx} + \int_1^2 ( \sqrt {5 - {x^2}} + x + 1)dx$$

$$ = \int_{ - 1}^1 {\sqrt {5 - {x^2}} dx} + \int_1^2 {\sqrt {5 - {x^2}} dx} + \int_{ - 1}^1 {(x - 1)dx} + \int_1^2 {( - x + 1)dx} $$

$$ = \int_{ - 1}^2 {\sqrt {5 - {x^2}} dx} + \left[ {{{{x^2}} \over 2} - x} \right]_{ - 1}^1 + \left[ { - {{{x^2}} \over 2} + x} \right]_1^2$$

$$ = \left[ {{1 \over 2} \times \sqrt {5 - {x^2}} + {5 \over 2}{{\sin }^{ - 1}}{x \over {\sqrt 5 }}} \right]_{ - 1}^2 - {5 \over 2}$$

$$ = {1 \over 2} + {5 \over 2}{\sin ^{ - 1}}{2 \over {\sqrt 5 }} + 1 + {5 \over 2}{\sin ^{ - 1}}{1 \over {\sqrt 5 }} - {5 \over 2}$$

$$ = - {1 \over 2} + {5 \over 2}{\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {{2 \over {\sqrt 5 }} \times \sqrt {1 - {1 \over 5}} + {1 \over {\sqrt 5 }}\sqrt {1 - {4 \over 5}} } \right)$$

$$ = - {1 \over 2} + {5 \over 2}{\sin ^{ - 1}}(1) = {{5\pi } \over 2} - {1 \over 2}$$ sq units

content: Let S be the set of points, whose abscissae and ordinates are natural numbers. Let P $$ \in $$ S, such that the sum of the distance of P from (8, 0) and (0, 12) is minimum among all elements in S. Then, the number of such points P and S is
explanation:
Sum of distances of point P from point (8, 0) and (0, 12) will be minimum, if points are collinear.

$$ \therefore $$ Equation of line at point (8, 0) and (0, 12).

$${x \over 8} + {y \over {12}} = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {y \over {12}} = 1 - {x \over 8}$$

$$ \Rightarrow y = 12 - {x \over 8} \times 12$$

$$ \Rightarrow y = 12 - {3 \over 2}x$$

$$ \therefore $$ Points (x, y) = (2, 9), (4, 6) and (6, 3). Hence, the number of such points P in S is 3.

content: If x is a positive real number different from 1 such that log<sub>a</sub>x, log<sub>b</sub>x, log<sub>c</sub>x are in AP, then
explanation:
Given, logax = logbx and logcx are in AP.

$$ \therefore $$ 2logbx = logax + logcx

$$ \Rightarrow 2{\log _b}x = {1 \over {{{\log }_x}a}} + {1 \over {{{\log }_x}c}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {2 \over {{{\log }_x}b}} = {{{{\log }_x}ac} \over {{{\log }_x}a{{\log }_x}c}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2{\log _x}c = {{{{\log }_x}b} \over {{{\log }_x}a}}({\log _x}ac)$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2{\log _x}c = {\log _a}b.{\log _x}ac$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\log _x}{C^2} = {\log _x}{(ac)^{{{\log }_a}b}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${c^2} = {(ac)^{{{\log }_a}b}}$$

content: If z<sub>1</sub>, z<sub>2</sub>, z<sub>3</sub> are imaginary numbers such that $$|{z_1}|\, = \,|{z_2}|\, = \,|{z_3}|\, = \,\left| {{1 \over {{z_1}}} + {1 \over {{z_2}}} + {1 \over {{z_3}}}} \right|\, = \,1$$, then $$|{z_1} + {z_2} + {z_3}|$$ is
explanation:
We have,

$$|{z_1}|\, = \,|{z_2}|\, = \,|{z_3}|\, = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow |{z_1}{|^2}\, = \,|{z_2}{|^2}\, = \,|{z_3}{|^2}\, = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {z_1}{\overline z _1} = {z_2}{\overline z _2} = {z_3}{\overline z _3} = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over {{z_1}}} = {\overline z _1},{1 \over {{z_2}}} = {\overline z _2},{1 \over {{z_3}}} = {\overline z _3}$$

Now, $$\left| {{1 \over {{z_1}}} + {1 \over {{z_2}}} + {1 \over {{z_3}}}} \right|\, = \,1$$

$$ \Rightarrow \left| {{{\overline z }_1} + {{\overline z }_2} + {{\overline z }_3}} \right|\, = 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow |\overline {{z_1} + {z_2} + {z_3}} |\, = \,1$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$|{z_1} + {z_2} + {z_3}|\, = 1$$

content: The number of ways in which the letters of the word ARRANGE can be permuted such that the R's occur together, is
explanation:
Given word is 'ARRANGE'.

If R's occur together, number of letters in word = 6

To arrange R's together number of ways = $${{2!} \over {2!}}$$ = 1

$$ \therefore $$ Number of ways to permuted 'ARRANGE' = $${{6!} \over {2!}}$$

content: $$1 + {}^n{C_1}\cos \theta  + {}^n{C_2}\cos 2\theta  + ... + {}^n{C_n}\cos n\theta $$ equals
If the function f : R $$ \to $$ R is defined by f(x) = (x<sup>2</sup> + 1)<sup>35</sup>, $$\forall $$ x$$ \in $$R, then f is
explanation:
Given f(x) = (x2 + 1)35, $$\forall$$x $$\in$$R.

Since, f(x) is even function.

Hence, the function is not one-one.

And f(x) > 0, $$\forall$$ x $$\in$$ R.

Hence, the function is not onto.

$$ \therefore $$ Given, the function is neither one-one nor onto.

content: The value of $$\cos 15^\circ \cos 7{{1^\circ } \over 2}\sin 7{{1^\circ } \over 2}$$ is
explanation:
We have, $$\cos 15^\circ .\cos 7{{1^\circ } \over 2}.\sin 7{{1^\circ } \over 2}$$

$$ = {1 \over 2}\cos 15^\circ \left( {2\cos 7{{1^\circ } \over 2}.\sin 7{{1^\circ } \over 2}} \right)$$

$$ = {1 \over 2}\cos 15^\circ \left[ {\sin 2\left( {7{{1^\circ } \over 2}} \right)} \right]$$ [$$ \because $$ 2sin $$\theta$$ . cos$$\theta$$ = sin2$$\theta$$]

$$ = {1 \over 2}\cos 15^\circ \sin 15^\circ $$

$$ = {1 \over 2} \times {1 \over 2}(2\sin 15^\circ \cos 15^\circ )$$

$$ = {1 \over 2}\sin 30^\circ = {1 \over 4} \times {1 \over 2} = {1 \over 8}$$

content: The smallest positive root of the equation tan x $$-$$ x = 0 lies in
explanation:
Given equation is

tan x $$-$$ x = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ tan x = x

Solutions are abscissae of points of intersection of the curves y = tan x and y = x. From the figure, it is clearly visible that solution lies in $$\left( {\pi ,{{3\pi } \over 2}} \right)$$

content: The points ($$-$$a, $$-$$b), (a, b), (0, 0) and (a<sup>2</sup>, ab), a $$ \ne $$ 0, b $$ \ne $$ 0 are always
The locus of the point of intersection of the straight lines $${x \over a} + {y \over b} = K$$ and $${x \over a} - {y \over b} = {1 \over K}$$, where K is a non-zero real variable, is given by
explanation:
Given equations of straight lines

$${x \over a} + {y \over b} = K$$ .... (i)

and $${x \over a} - {y \over b} = {1 \over K}$$ .... (ii)

Let the point of intersection be ($$\alpha$$, $$\beta$$).

So, from Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get,

$${\alpha \over a} + {\beta \over b} = K$$

and $${\alpha \over a} - {\beta \over b} = {1 \over K}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\left( {{\alpha \over a}} \right)^2} - {\left( {{\beta \over b}} \right)^2} = 1 \Rightarrow {{{\alpha ^2}} \over {{a^2}}} - {{{\beta ^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$

$$ \therefore $$ Locus $${{{x^2}} \over {{a^2}}} - {{{y^2}} \over {{b^2}}} = 1$$, which is the equation of a hyperbola.

content: A straight line joining the points (1, 1, 1) and (0, 0, 0) intersects the plane 2x + 2y + z = 10 at
explanation:
Equation of line joining the points (1, 1, 1) and (0, 0, 0) is

$${{x - 0} \over {1 - 0}} = {{y - 0} \over {1 - 0}} = {{z - 0} \over {1 - 0}} = \lambda $$ (say)

$$ \Rightarrow $$ x = y = z = $$\lambda$$

So, the point is ($$\lambda$$, $$\lambda$$, $$\lambda$$).

The point intersects the plane 2x + 2y + z = 10

$$ \therefore $$ 2($$\lambda$$) + 2($$\lambda$$) + $$\lambda$$ = 10

$$ \Rightarrow $$ 5$$\lambda$$ = 10

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$\lambda$$ = 2

Hence, the point is (2, 2, 2).

content: Angle between the planes x + y + 2z = 6 and 2x $$-$$ y + z = 9 is
explanation:
Equations of planes are

x + y + 2z $$-$$ 6 = 0 .... (i)

and 2x $$-$$ y + z $$-$$ 9 = 0 .... (ii)

$$ \therefore $$ $$\cos \theta = {{{a_1}{a_2} + {b_1}{b_2} + {c_1}{c_2}} \over {\sqrt {a_1^2 + b_1^2 + c_1^2} \sqrt {a_2^2 + b_2^2 + c_2^2} }}$$

$$ = {{1 \times 2 + 1 \times ( - 1) + 2 \times 1} \over {\sqrt {1 + 1 + 4} \sqrt {4 + 1 + 1} }}$$

$$ = {{2 - 1 + 2} \over {\sqrt 6 \times \sqrt 6 }} = {3 \over 6} = {1 \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \cos \left( {{\pi \over 3}} \right)$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$\theta = {\pi \over 3}$$

content: If f(x) is an odd differentiable function defined on ($$-$$$$\infty $$, $$\infty $$) such that f'(3) = 2, then f'($$-$$3) is equal to
explanation:
Given that f(x) is an odd differentiable function.

Then, f($$-$$x) = $$-$$ f(x)

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$-$$f'($$-$$x) = $$-$$f'(x)

$$ \Rightarrow $$ f'($$-$$x) = f'(x) .... (i)

Put x = 3 in Eq. (i), we get

f'($$-$$3) = f'(3)

$$ \therefore $$ f'($$-$$3) = 2 [$$ \because $$ f'(3) = 2]

content: $$\int {{{\log \sqrt x } \over {3x}}} dx$$ is equal to
explanation:
Let $$l = \int {{{\log \sqrt x } \over {3x}}} dx$$

Again, let $$\log \sqrt x = z \Rightarrow {1 \over {2x}}dx = dz$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$l = \int {{{2z} \over 3}} dz = {2 \over 3}\int z \,dz$$

$${2 \over 3}.{{{z^2}} \over 2} + C = {1 \over 3}{(\log \sqrt x )^2} + C$$

content: The equation x<sup>3</sup> $$-$$ yx<sup>2</sup> + x $$-$$ y = 0 represents

options.content: a hyperbola and two straight lines
a parabola and two straight lines
a straight line and a circle

explanation:
Given equation is

x3 $$-$$ yx2 + x $$-$$ y = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ x2 (x $$-$$ y) + (x $$-$$ y) = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ (x2 + 1) (x $$-$$ y) = 0

Now, x2 + 1 $$ \ne $$ 0

and x $$-$$ y = 0

$$ \therefore $$ x = y

So, the equation represents a straight line.

content: The number of points at which the function f(x) = max {a $$-$$ x, a + x, b}, $$-$$ $$\infty $$ < x < $$\infty $$, 0 < a < b cannot be differentiable, is
explanation:
Graph of f(x)



There are two sharp turns. Hence, f(x) cannot be differentiable at two points.

content: For non-zero vectors a and b, if | a + b | < | a $$-$$ b |, then a and b are
explanation:
Given, $$|a + b| < |a - b|$$

Now,

$$|a + b{|^2} < |a - b{|^2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow (a + b).(a + b) < (a - b)(a - b)$$

$$ \Rightarrow |a{|^2} + |b{|^2} + 2a.\,b < |a{|^2} + |b{|^2} - 2a.\,b$$

$$ \Rightarrow 4a.\,b < 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow a.\,b < 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow |a||b|\cos \theta < 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow \cos \theta < 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow \theta \in $$ obtuse angle

$$ \therefore $$ a and b are inclined at an obtuse angle.

content: Find the general solution of $$\sec \theta + 1 = (2 + \sqrt 3 )\tan \theta $$.
explanation:
$$\sec \theta + 1 = (2 + \sqrt 3 )\tan \theta $$

Squaring both sides, we get

$${(\sec \theta + 1)^2} = {(2 + \sqrt 3 )^2}{\tan ^2}\theta $$

$$ \Rightarrow {(\sec \theta + 1)^2} = (4 + 4\sqrt 3 + 3)({\sec ^2}\theta - 1)$$

$$ \Rightarrow {(\sec \theta + 1)^2} - (7 + 4\sqrt 3 )({\sec ^2}\theta - 1) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {(\sec \theta + 1)^2} - (7 + 4\sqrt 3 )(\sec \theta + 1)(\sec \theta - 1) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (\sec \theta + 1)[\sec \theta + 1 - (7 + 4\sqrt 3 )(\sec \theta - 1)] = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (\sec \theta + 1)[(8 + 4\sqrt 3 ) - (6 + 4\sqrt 3 )\sec \theta ] = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sec \theta + 1 = 0$$ or $$(8 + 4\sqrt 3 ) - (6 + 4\sqrt 3 )\sec \theta = 0$$

If $$\sec \theta + 1 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow \cos \theta = - 1 = \cos \pi \Rightarrow \theta = 2n\pi \pm \pi $$ ..... (i)

If $$(8 + 4\sqrt 3 ) - (6 + 4\sqrt 3 )\sec \theta = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sec \theta = {{8 + 4\sqrt 3 } \over {6 + 4\sqrt 3 }} = {{4(2 + \sqrt 3 )} \over {2\sqrt 3 (2 + \sqrt 3 )}} = {2 \over {\sqrt 3 }}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \cos \theta = {{\sqrt 3 } \over 2} = \cos {\pi \over 6}$$

$$ \Rightarrow \theta = 2n\pi \pm {\pi \over 6}$$

$$\because$$ angles obtained from $$\theta = 2n\pi - {\pi \over 6}$$ is not satisfying the given equation

$$\therefore$$ $$\theta = 2n\pi \pm \pi $$, $$2n\pi + {\pi \over 6}$$, $$n \in I$$

content: Show that $${{\sin \theta } \over {\cos 3\theta }} + {{\sin 3\theta } \over {\cos 9\theta }} + {{\sin 9\theta } \over {\cos 27\theta }} = {1 \over 2}(\tan 27\theta - \tan \theta )$$
explanation:
$${{\sin \theta } \over {\cos 3\theta }} + {{\sin 3\theta } \over {\cos 9\theta }} + {{\sin 9\theta } \over {\cos 27\theta }} = {1 \over 2}(\tan 27\theta - \tan \theta )$$

Here, $${{\sin \theta } \over {\cos 3\theta }} = {1 \over 2}{{2\sin \theta \,.\,\cos \theta } \over {\cos 3\theta \,.\,\cos \theta }} = {1 \over 2}{{\sin 2\theta } \over {\cos 3\theta \,.\,\cos \theta }}$$

$$ = {1 \over 2}{{\sin (3\theta - \theta )} \over {\cos 3\theta \,.\,\cos \theta }} = {1 \over 2}(\tan 3\theta - \tan \theta )$$ ..... (i)

Similarly, $${{\sin 3\theta } \over {\cos 9\theta }} = {1 \over 2}(\tan 9\theta - \tan 3\theta )$$ (Replace $$\theta$$ by 3$$\theta$$ in (i))

$${{\sin 9\theta } \over {\cos 27\theta }} = {1 \over 2}(\tan 27\theta - \tan 9\theta )$$ (Replace $$\theta$$ by 9$$\theta$$ in (i))

$$\therefore$$ L.H.S. $$ = {1 \over 2}[(\tan 3\theta - \tan \theta ) + (\tan 9\theta - \tan 3\theta ) + (\tan 27\theta - \tan 9\theta )]$$

$$ = {1 \over 2}(\tan 27\theta - \tan \theta ) = $$ R.H.S.

content: Prov that the equation $$\cos 2x + a\sin x = 2a - 7$$ possesses a solution if 2 $$\le$$ a $$\le$$ 6.
explanation:
$$\because$$ $$\cos 2x + a\sin x = 2a - 7$$

$$\therefore$$ $$(1 - 2{\sin ^2}x) + a\sin x - 2a + 7 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2{\sin ^2}x - a\sin x + 2a - 8 = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow \sin x = {{a\, \pm \,\sqrt {{{( - a)}^2} - 4\,.\,2\,.\,(2a - 8)} } \over {2(2)}} = {{a\, \pm \,(a - 8)} \over 4}$$

$$ = {a \over 2} - 2,2$$

$$\because$$ $$\sin x \ne 2$$

$$\therefore$$ $$\sin x = {a \over 2} - 2$$

$$\because$$ $$ - 1 \le \sin x \le 1$$

$$\therefore$$ $$ - 1 \le {a \over 2} - 2 \le 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow - 2 \le a - 4 \le 2$$

$$\therefore$$ $$2 \le a \le 6$$

content: Find the values of x, ($$-$$ $$\pi$$, < x < $$\pi$$, x $$\ne$$ 0) satisfying the equation, $${8^{1 + |\cos x| + |{{\cos }^2}x| + ......\infty }} = {4^3}$$.
explanation:
$$\because$$ $${8^{1 + |\cos x| + |{{\cos }^2}x| + ......\infty }} = {4^3}$$

$$\therefore$$ $${8^{{1 \over {1 - |\cos x|}}}} = {2^6} \Rightarrow {2^{{3 \over {1 - |\cos x|}}}} = {2^6}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {3 \over {1 - |\cos x|}} = 6 \Rightarrow 1 - |\cos x| = {1 \over 2} \Rightarrow \cos x = \pm {1 \over 2}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$x = {\pi \over 6}, - {\pi \over 6},{{2\pi } \over 3}, - {{2\pi } \over 3}$$ ($$\because$$ $$ - \pi < x < \pi $$)

content: If the equation $\sin ^4 x-(p+2) \sin ^2 x-(p+3)=0$ has a solution, the $p$ must lie in the interval
explanation:
Given the equation $\sin ^4 x-(p+2) \sin ^2 x-(p+3)=0$ has a solution.

Let $y=\sin ^2 x$. Since, $0 \leq \sin ^2 x \leq 1$, we must have $0 \leq y \leq 1$.

The equation becomes a quadratic in

$$ y: y^2-(p+2) y-(p+3)=0 $$

Solve for $y$ using the quadratic formula

$$ \begin{aligned} & y=\frac{-B \pm \sqrt{B^2-4 A C}}{2 A} \\ & y=\frac{(p+2) \pm \sqrt{(p+2)^2-4(1)(-(p+3))}}{2} \\ & y=\frac{(p+2) \pm \sqrt{(p+4)^2}}{2} \\ & y=\frac{(p+2) \pm|p+4|}{2} \end{aligned} $$

Consider two cases for $|p+4|$

Case I $p+4 \geq 0 \Rightarrow p \geq-4$

The two roots are

$$ y_1=\frac{p+2+(p+4)}{2}=\frac{2 p+6}{2}=p+3 $$

$$ y_2=\frac{p+2-(p+4)}{2}=\frac{-2}{2}=-1 . $$

For a solutions to exist, at least one root must be in the interval $[0,1]$

$y_2=-1$ is not in $[0,1]$

for $y_1=p+3$ to be in $[0,1]$

$$ \begin{aligned} 0 & \leq p+3 \leq 1 \\ 0 & \leq p+3 \Rightarrow p \geq-3 \\ p+3 & \leq 1 \Rightarrow p \leq-2 . \end{aligned} $$

Combining these with $p \geq-4$, the valid range for $p$ is $[-3,-2]$

Case II $p+4<0 \Rightarrow p<-4$

The two roots are

$$ \begin{aligned} y_1 & =\frac{p+2-(p+4)}{2}=\frac{-2}{2}=-1 \\ y_2 & =\frac{p+2+(-(p+4))}{2} \\ & =\frac{p+2-p-4}{2}=\frac{-2}{2}=-1 \end{aligned} $$

Both roots are $y=-1$, which is not in $[0,1]$. Thus there are no solution for $p<-4$.

Combining the results from both cases, the only interval for $p$ that yields a valid solutions is $[-3,-2]$

content: If $0 \leq a, b \leq 3$ and the equation $x^2+4+3 \cos (a x+b)=2 x$ has real solutions, then the value of $(a+b)$ is
explanation:
Given, $0 \leq a, b \leq 3$ and the equation $x^2+4+3 \cos (a x+b)=2 x$ has a real solution.

Rearrange the equation.

$$ \begin{gathered} x^2-2 x+4+3 \cos (a x+b)=0 \\ (x-1)^2+3+3 \cos (a x+b)=0 \\ (x-1)^2+3(1+\cos (a x+b))=0 \end{gathered} $$

For this equation to have a real solution both terms must be zero, as $(x-1)^2 \geq 0$ and $3(1+\cos (a x+b)) \geq 0$ (since $-1 \leq \cos (\theta)$ ) $\leq 1,0 \leq 1+\cos (\theta) \leq 2$ ).

So, we must have $(x-1)^2=0$, which implies $x=1$

And $\quad 3(1+\cos (a x+b))=0$, which implies $1+\cos (a x+b)=0$, leading to $\cos (a x+b)=-1$.

Substitute $x=1$ into the second condition.

$$ \cos (a(1)+b)=\cos (a+b)=-1 $$

For $\cos (\theta)=-1, \theta$ must be of the form $(2 n+1) \pi$ for some integer $n$.

So, $a+b=(2 n+1) \pi$.

Given constraints are $0 \leq a \leq 3$ and $0 \leq b \leq 3$.

Therefore, $0 \leq a+b \leq 6$.

We need to find values of $(2 n+1) \pi$ that fall within the range $[0,6]$.

If $n=0, a+b=\pi \approx 3.14159$. This value is in $[0,6]$.

If $n=1, a+b=3 \pi \approx 9.42$. This value is outside $[0,6]$

If $n=-1, a+b=-\pi \approx-314159$. This values is outside $[0,6]$

The only possible value for $a+b$ that satisfies the conditions is $\pi$.

content: The solution set of the equation $\left(x \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right) \tan (\pi \tan x)=\cot (\pi \cot x)$, is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { } \tan (\pi \tan x)=\cot (\pi \cot x)=\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{2}=\pi \cot x\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \pi \tan x=\frac{\pi}{2}-\pi \cot x \\ & \Rightarrow \tan x+\cot x=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \tan x+\cot x=\frac{1}{2} \tan x+\frac{1}{\tan x}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \tan ^2 x+2=\tan x \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \tan ^2 x-\tan x+2=0 \end{aligned} $$

This is quadratic in $\tan x$

$$ \begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad \tan x & =\frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1-4.4}}{4} \\ & =\frac{1 \pm \sqrt{-15}}{2} \end{aligned} $$

Which are not real solution set $=\phi$.

content: If the first and (2n $$-$$ 1)th terms of an AP, GP and HP are equal and their nth terms are respectively a, b, c, then always
explanation:
$$ \because $$ a, b, and c are respectively the AM, GM and HM of first and (2n $$-$$ 1)th terms.

and also we know,

AM $$ \ge $$ GM $$ \ge $$ HM and AM . HM = GM2

$$ \therefore $$ a $$ \ge $$ b $$ \ge $$ c and a . c = b2

content: If the parabola x<sup>2</sup> = ay makes an intercept of length $$\sqrt {40} $$ units on the line y $$-$$ 2x = 1, then, a is equal to
explanation:
Given, equation of the parabola

x2 = ay

i.e. $$y = {{{x^2}} \over a}$$ .... (i)

and the equation of line

y $$-$$ 2x = 1

i.e. y = 2x + 1 ..... (ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$ \Rightarrow {{{x^2}} \over a} = 2x + 1$$

$$ \Rightarrow {x^2} = 2ax + a$$

$$ \Rightarrow {x^2} - 2ax - a = 0$$ .... (iii)

$$ \therefore $$ $$x = {{2a \pm \sqrt {4{a^2} + 4a} } \over 2}$$

$$ = {{2(a \pm \sqrt {{a^2} + a} )} \over 2} = a \pm \sqrt {{a^2} + a} $$

Points of intersection of the parabola and the line are

$$(a + \sqrt {{a^2} + a} ,2(a + \sqrt {{a^2} + a} ) + 1)$$

and $$(a - \sqrt {{a^2} + a} ,2(a - \sqrt {{a^2} + a} + 1)$$

$$ \because $$ Distance between the points = $$\sqrt {40} $$

$$ \therefore $$ $$\sqrt {40} = \sqrt {{{(2\sqrt {{a^2} + a} )}^2} + {{(4\sqrt {{a^2} + a} )}^2}} $$

$$ \Rightarrow 40 = 4({a^2} + a) + 16({a^2} + a)$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2 = {a^2} + a \Rightarrow a = 1, - 2$$
